story:
The Vreesek conquered Earth and brought their gods. They don’t plunder; they plant temple-embassies and let us “rule” ourselves under their shadow. Their high priest and ambassador—the iVress—is our Elder. They seek converts without preaching. Mistakes get punished. We remember Whidbey Island’s vaporized light.

They need us to keep order. I’m Detective Jessica Cho, SPD—now a Vreesek-whisperer through a bud-buzz earbud you blink on and off. Useful for them, not for me and Marcus with our deaf son, Brien.

There’s a dead Vreesek child outside the Seattle embassy. Small. Yellow scales turning gray. Teal fluid pooling in the rain. In his hands, a silvery icon like a toy: a tiny child nested inside a hollow cube shell. The Vreesek guard buzzes: the iVress will see you.

He’s taller than the vids, ochre and gold scales bristling, naked but for a glittering chain bearing an innocent face. His mind-voice roars through the bud: a mother lost her child. she accuses me of the disposition. find the truth. return the shokrah. restore the peace. remember your whidbey island.

They bring in the mother—sleek scales, scarlet and orange—shaking before him, her eyes flat to me. He leaves us.

She buzzes: My child. My most precious thing, stolen from me.

How? By whom?

She waits, then: He was on the balcony. The iVress wanted him gone.

Did the Elder push him?

The child was disobedient. He stole the shokrah.

Silence.

On the balcony, I find two smudges on the wide rail, a scatter of gray scales. Back inside, she’s gone. I ride the elevator down, stare up into the drizzle at the high ledge. Would a child be so foolish? Could a mother be so calm?

Home. Garlic and oregano. Marcus wipes his apron, grins. Brien bounces on the couch, signs ten new words, signs chocolate. I scold the price and hug him anyway. After prayers I tuck him in with his snugglebear. In the dim light he is a small angel. I cannot imagine losing him.

Morning. The coroner hands me the silvery “toy.” Not a toy—the shokrah. The face of a holy child gazes up. I went back to the embassy-temple, then out to the balcony again. The two smudges and the scales were cleaned up. I peered over the railing.

He was face-up. 

To a guard: Who uses this room when the iVress is absent?

No one but his family.

And the dead child? The iVress’s?

All who share the shokrah are his children. He fingers his own icon, the tiny child inside a rounded cube. This Child commands us to rule the galaxy.

Bring me the mother.

She stands steady. You have news?

I open my hand. This is yours.

A high, whistled sound, then she snatches the shokrah. Thank all the gods! The shokrah is returned to me, and I am returned to the Vreet, the Holy Child!

Tell me about your son.

She stares. He was disobedient to the Vreet and the iVress.

He took your Vreet, and with it, your place.

How did you know?

He was looking at you when you pushed him off for stealing. His mother.

A choking rasp. The gods are to be obeyed, always. The Vreet is everything. The Vreesek is all.

I slip out the earbud so my thoughts won’t betray me. The iVress ordered truth and peace. The truth burns. Peace is a bargain. A boy is dead; his mother holds her place. Justice has no place here that does not draw fire.

That evening, the sun paints wet streets gold and red. My hand rests on Brien’s hair. He looks up, returns to his silent game.

Perhaps this is enough—that we live, I whisper.

“It is enough,” Marcus says.
